topic: Someone receives a friend request from a childhood bully
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification blinked on the screen, a jarring intrusion in the otherwise quiet evening. A profile picture loaded: a familiar, sneering face that used to dominate the hallways of middle school. A jolt went through Maya’s chest, tightening her breath until it felt shallow. *Liam*. Her childhood tormentor. The request was there, simple and utterly unwelcome. Her fingers hovered over the 'Accept' button, a cold knot forming in her stomach. She minimized the message, focusing intently on the intricate pattern of the rug beneath her feet, trying to anchor herself against the sudden internal tremor. Every muscle in her jaw felt tight, a hard line she desperately tried to maintain. What if he just wanted attention? The thought spiraled, bringing a wave of nausea that made her grip the edge of the desk until her knuckles were white.

[story 2]

I stared at my phone as if it were a venomous insect. Another request. It was him. Mark. The name alone brought a rush of heat to my cheeks, a flush that spread down my neck. My hands started to shake, a slight, involuntary tremor that made typing feel clumsy and slow. Why now? After all this time, after building a life that felt solid and predictable, this ghost from my past decided to reappear. I paced the small room, the floorboards protesting beneath my bare feet with every movement. My heart hammered against my ribs, a frantic rhythm I couldn't seem to slow down. I took a deep, ragged breath, trying to force the frantic energy down, but it just churned, leaving me feeling utterly unmoored and hyper-aware of every sound in the apartment.

[story 3]

The chime sounded, sharp and intrusive. It was a friend request. My gaze snagged on the avatar—that familiar, mocking smirk. A deep, unpleasant clench settled low in my abdomen, making it hard to draw a full breath. I didn't move for a full minute, just staring at the glowing icon. My palms began to sweat, a clammy dampness that seeped into my skin. I wanted to throw the device across the room, to shatter the glass screen, but I held still. My mind raced, cataloging every possible negative scenario: confrontation, ridicule, a forced interaction. The air in the room seemed thick, heavy, pressing down on my shoulders until they ached. I finally reached for the phone, my movements stiff and deliberate, the decision feeling monumental and terrifyingly difficult.
